flashcards.front: What is disk scheduling in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Disk scheduling is the process of managing and ordering disk I/O requests to minimize seek time, rotational latency, and maximize throughput.

flashcards.front: Describe the FCFS disk scheduling algorithm.
flashcards.back: FCFS (First Come First Served) services disk requests in the order they arrive. It is simple but may lead to high average seek time if requests are scattered.

flashcards.front: What is the SSTF disk scheduling algorithm?
flashcards.back: SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) selects the request closest to the current head position to minimize seek distance. It reduces average seek time but may cause starvation for distant requests.

flashcards.front: Explain the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm.
flashcards.back: SCAN, also known as the elevator algorithm, moves the disk head from one end to the other, servicing requests along the way, then reverses direction. It provides low average seek time and avoids starvation.

flashcards.front: How does C-SCAN differ from SCAN?
flashcards.back: C-SCAN (Circular SCAN) services requests in one direction only; when it reaches the end, it jumps back to the beginning without servicing requests on the return, ensuring more uniform wait times.
LOOK is similar to SCAN but only travels to the last request in each direction, avoiding unnecessary head movement to the disk's end.
C-LOOK is like C-SCAN but only goes to the last request in each direction before jumping back, minimizing seek time while maintaining circular service.

flashcards.front: Which disk scheduling algorithm is known as the elevator algorithm and why?
flashcards.back: The SCAN algorithm is called the elevator algorithm because it moves the disk head back and forth like an elevator, servicing requests in both directions.
